what_it_does: Synthesizes a 7-agent research wave into an evidence base for the Universal Projection instrument, mapping existing company code and design substrates to projection capabilities, listing genuinely new build items, flagging contradictions and traps, and recording unresolved ratification questions for the originator.
observations: Originated by Tim Geldard; all derived work is attributed to him.
Produced by a 7-agent research wave on 2026-06-13 consuming ~770k tokens, reading stored design against actual code, registries, embedding substrate, prior loop docs, and design system.
Declares a one-way correction flow: synthesis → guide → criteria, never the reverse.
Round 1 frames the projection as a geometric face of already-built machinery, with 'free the bindings' collapsing four hardcoded resolve paths into one parameterized instrument.
Round 2 is a reuse map that pairs each capability with exact existing machinery such as runtime/projection.py, runtime/bridge.py, suite.py, vector_index.py, and BindingRegistry.
Round 3 lists net-new build items, led by a committed acceptance suite for projection invariants.

Round 4 highlights real traps: name collisions between projection.py (instrument), projections.py / projections/ (lens registry), and tests/projections_acceptance.py (which tests the lens registry, not the instrument).
Round 4 also flags a FORM hold-out in LatticeView.tsx using dead --accent / --ink-dim tokens and ~37 literals in app.css.

Round 4 marks several apparent gaps as intentional design: forbidden/divergent points, 'nothing ratified' footers, per-point hue as geometry, optional coherence machinery, and the forager not subsuming the instrument.

Round 5 records model calls / ratification gates that are Tim's to answer, including the register-of-registries prime/divisor lattice, two-gravity anchors, the two axes, k dimension, spectrum, scale prioritization, and the 20/80 dial.
standouts: The instrument is described as not new machinery but the geometric face of four existing resolve paths that share the same call with hardcoded bindings.
The two-gravity separator is identified as the highest near-term value ability, yet it is gated on the originator setting the anchor poles.
The name-collision trap around tests/projections_acceptance.py is emphasized as a source of false confidence that the instrument is tested when it is not.
The event→registry-row edge is called the keystone primitive and is explicitly flagged as not built; it unblocks angle-from-registry and order-from-typed-edges.
Round 5 model calls are framed as ratification gates, not build tasks.
conventions: Uses numbered rounds with bold, all-caps headings to separate categories.
Cites exact file paths, function names, and approximate line numbers for grounding.
Attributes origin and derived work to a named author.
Maintains a neutral, file-local stance and avoids ranking competing approaches.
Distinguishes reuse, net-new, trap, stub, and intentional-gap states explicitly.
Declares an authoritative, unidirectional correction flow.
Mixes descriptive synthesis with prescriptive build signals such as 'First build item'.
concerns: Relies on approximate line numbers that may drift as code changes.
Dense coined terminology may reduce accessibility for future readers.
Presents some agent-interpreted findings as ground truth.
The noted name collisions, while flagged, remain a structural readability risk.
Important quality-debt details about LatticeView.tsx are buried inside Round 4.
notes: A research-to-build map that argues most of the Universal Projection already exists in the codebase, precisely names what is genuinely missing, and leaves the hard design choices as originator-ratification gates.
questions: Which exact commit or version of each cited file was inspected by the research wave?
What is the precise signature and behavior of resolve(centre, spaces, n, k, audience, exhale)?
How will the two-gravity anchor sets be chosen and maintained over time?
What test harness and invariants will the committed acceptance suite enforce?
If the register-of-registries is ratified as a prime/divisor lattice, what is the formal mapping?
What is the intended relationship between the event-store universe and the curated corpus beyond the current separation?
purpose_vs_actual: Matches: the file presents itself as the evidence base for the instrument→ability buildout and delivers exactly that — a grounded synthesis, reuse map, net-new list, traps, and ratification questions.
apparent_themes: Time as a privileged, involuntary address
Clear separation between instrument, lens registry, and tests
Originator ratification before final design hardening
intention_signals: The file is trying to convert a large, multi-agent research effort into a build-ready plan for a universal projection instrument, demonstrating that the company has already built most of the required machinery and surfacing only the primitives and decisions that still need originator attention.
novelty: A research synthesis organized as explicit rounds (step-up opportunities, reuse map, net-new, contradictions/traps, model calls), with line-numbered code grounding, named attribution, a declared correction-flow contract, and first-class treatment of design traps and ratification gates.
contribution: A grounded inventory mapping existing code mechanisms to projection capabilities, a precise list of genuinely unbuilt primitives, a catalog of name collisions and false-confidence traps, and a set of originator-ratification questions that prevent premature hardening.
summary_for_embedding: A research synthesis for the Universal Projection instrument that maps existing company machinery to projection capabilities, lists net-new build items, flags name collisions and FORM debt, and records unresolved ratification questions from a 7-agent research wave.
intention_for_embedding: To provide a build-prep evidence base showing that the projection instrument is mostly reuse of existing code, while isolating the exact gaps and design decisions that require originator ratification.
